agent1_snippets.title: Informed Consent -- Issues of Concern -- Lack of Patient Comprehension
agent1_snippets.content: Tools such as the teach-back method or test/feedback method can be used to assess whether patients comprehend the risks, benefits, and alternatives of their treatment. The teach-back method can help both patients and clinicians concentrate on the essential aspects of the information. To encourage active patient participation, engagement techniques and open-ended questions may be used to gather insights into patients' needs and preferences. Patients should be encouraged to ask questions to foster a more interactive dialogue. Interactive media and graphical tools can improve shared decision-making and effectively assess and present risks. Future research should continue to explore interactive interventions, given that these methods appear superior in improving patient comprehension. [20] [21] [20]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788
agent2_snippets.content: Amniotomy at delivery is an artificial rupture of the membranes (تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي), a deliberate obstetric procedure in which a clinician intentionally breaks the amniotic sac to accelerate labor. By creating a controlled opening, amniotomy facilitates the descent of the fetus, enhances the effectiveness of uterine contractions, and allows for more accurate fetal monitoring. The technique is typically employed when labor is progressing slowly, when there is a need to augment contractions, or to expedite delivery for maternal or fetal indications. While amniotomy can shorten the duration of labor and reduce the need for pharmacologic induction, it also carries potential risks such as infection, cord prolapse, or excessive fetal head compression, and therefore is performed after careful assessment of the clinical situation and informed consent. Proper selection of candidates and adherence to aseptic technique are essential to maximize the benefits of this artificial membrane rupture in obstetric care.

agent2_snippets.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. agent2_snippets.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.
